context: chapter number three, 2 Thessalonians chapter number three, and I'll be looking at verse number 13 this evening.

context: But ye, brethren, be not weary in well-doing.
point_summary: Contrasted with the correction of disorderly members in verses 6–12, verse 13 issues a direct charge to faithful believers not to grow faint or exhausted in upright conduct.

context: but ye, brethren, be not weary in well-doing
point_summary: Paul's command "be not weary in well-doing" is addressed specifically to those already acting uprightly, with "weary" meaning to become utterly spiritless or exhausted in the pursuit of right conduct.

context: but ye, brethren, be not weary in well-doing
point_summary: Keeping Christ — not human acknowledgment or appreciation — as the object of one's service is the antidote to growing weary in well-doing.

context: when Paul writes to the believers in Thessalonica, he says, but ye brethren, be not weary in well doing.
point_summary: Serving in one's own strength inevitably produces weariness, but relying on God's power—renewing the inward man daily—is the means by which believers fulfill the command not to grow weary in well doing.

reference_text: But ye brethren, be not weary in well-doing
context: remember, what does he say? But ye brethren, be not weary in well-doing.
point_summary: Avoiding weariness in well-doing requires recognizing that spiritual strength to persevere comes not from personal resolve but from reliance on Christ's power, without whom nothing can be accomplished.

context: that is very similar to the truth that we see in 2 Thessalonians 3 in verse 13. But ye brethren, and he says be not weary in well doing.
point_summary: Avoiding weariness in well-doing requires relying entirely on God's power rather than one's own strength, since apart from Christ nothing can be accomplished.

context: Now, does that sound awfully familiar to our texts of scriptures from second Thessalonians three and verse 13? But ye brethren, be not weary in well-doing.
point_summary: The command not to grow weary in well-doing (2 Thess. 3:13) is grounded in the promise of Galatians 6:9 that a harvest will come in due season to those who do not faint.

context: but ye brethren be not weary in well-doing
point_summary: The promise of an eschatological harvest—"in due season we shall reap if we faint not"—grounds the command against weariness, with the reaping potentially extending beyond the believer's own lifetime into generational impact.

context: but I wanna note for you in 2 Thessalonians 1, the spiritual growth that we see in the church in Thessalonica
point_summary: Alongside the numeric growth seen in 1 Thessalonians 1, 2 Thessalonians 1 is cited as evidence that the Thessalonian church also experienced spiritual growth, which the sermon presents as equally vital and as the foundation that produces numeric growth.

context: we are bound to thank God always for you, brethren, as it is meat. Notice what he says, because your faith, because that your faith groweth exceedingly.
point_summary: Paul's commendation of the Thessalonian church highlights that true church growth is measured first by exceedingly growing faith and abounding mutual love, not by numerical increase.

context: In verse number 11 of 2 Thessalonians 3, the Bible says, for we hear that there are some which walk among you disorderly, working not at all, but are busy bodies.
point_summary: Idleness and busybody behavior condemned in 2 Thessalonians 3:11 extend beyond any particular marital status or age, indicting all believers who walk disorderly by meddling rather than working.

context: 2 Thessalonians chapter number one, in verse number three, 2 Thessalonians one in verse number three, Paul says, we are bound to thank God always for you, brethren, as it is meat. Why? Because that your faith groweth exceedingly and the charity of every one of you all toward each other aboundeth.
point_summary: The Thessalonians' exceedingly growing faith and abounding charity is presented as the direct fruit of properly receiving and understanding God's Word.

context: Now we beseech you, brethren, by the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ and by our gathering together unto Him.

context: He says, let no man deceive you by any means in verse number three.
point_summary: False teaching that contradicts apostolic instruction necessitates a pastoral response of comfort, correction, and instruction — all three of which Paul's second letter to Thessalonica was written to provide.

context: Disorderly Behavior, 2 Thessalonians 3 verses 6 through 10. As we look at the portion of Scripture, the Bible says in verse number six, now we command you.
point_summary: Paul's shift from beseeching and exhorting in 1 Thessalonians to commanding in 2 Thessalonians 3:6 signals that congregational withdrawal from disorderly members carries apostolic authority, not mere suggestion.

context: Now we command you brethren, in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, that ye withdraw yourselves from every brother that walketh disorderly, and not after the tradition which he received of us.
point_summary: Paul's shift from beseeching and exhorting in 1 Thessalonians to commanding in 3:6 signals that God's instruction here carries the weight of a directive, not a suggestion, leaving no ambiguity for the believer.

context: for yourselves know how you ought to follow us, for we behave not ourselves disorderly among you
point_summary: Paul's own conduct among the Thessalonians — working without being a burden — is presented as the behavioral standard believers are commanded to imitate, not merely admire.

context: neither did we eat any man's bread for naught, but wrought with labor and travail night and day, that we might not be chargeable to any of you.
point_summary: Paul's deliberate labor "night and day" among the Thessalonians is presented as a self-funded example, not mere necessity, intended to model the work ethic he now commands of them.

context: Not because we have not power, but to make ourselves an example unto you to follow us.
point_summary: Paul and his companions voluntarily forfeited their apostolic right to financial support in order to set a working example for the Thessalonian believers to imitate.

context: for even when we were with you, this we commanded you, that if any should not work, neither should he eat.
point_summary: Paul's command that the unwilling worker should not eat is presented here as a direct apostolic instruction given in person before being restated in writing, underscoring its binding authority.

context: you notice in verse number six that there were some within the church that were walking disorderly... it says that you withdraw yourselves from every brother that walketh disorderly
point_summary: Paul's command to withdraw from every brother walking disorderly identifies a present, actual problem in the Thessalonian church—not a hypothetical future one—with failure to work named as the specific disorderly behavior in view.

context: for even when we were with you this we commanded you so it was something that had been previously commanded that if any would not work neither should he eat
point_summary: The command "if any would not work, neither should he eat" targets those who *will not* work—not those who *cannot*—making willful idleness the specific disorderly behavior Paul addresses.

context: disorderly in verse 6
point_summary: The Greek word translated "disorderly" in 2 Thessalonians 3:6 is linguistically connected to "unruly" in 1 Thessalonians 5:14, revealing a deliberate thread between the two letters addressing the same persistent problem.

context: disorderly in verse number 7
point_summary: The Greek word translated "disorderly" in 2 Thessalonians 3:7 derives from the same root as "unruly" in 1 Thessalonians 5:14, linking the two letters and showing the problem had persisted without correction.

context: we saw in chapter 2 in verse number 2 they were telling him that the day of Christ was at hand
point_summary: False teaching that the day of Christ had already come led some Thessalonians to abandon work, illustrating how eschatological error produces practical disorder.

context: Paul makes it extremely clear in verse number six notice he says now we command you we made point of that earlier but he says we command you brethren notice in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ that ye withdraw yourselves from every brother that walketh disorderly and not after the tradition which he received of us
point_summary: Paul's command to "withdraw" from disorderly brethren is presented as a mandatory ecclesiastical step — issued in Christ's name — for believers who refuse to work in defiance of apostolic tradition.

context: he says in verse 14 if any man obey not our
point_summary: Church discipline for persistent disobedience to apostolic teaching requires social separation—not as punishment treating the offender as an enemy, but as shame-inducing admonition directed toward a brother.

context: These believers, unfortunately, that are being spoken of in 2 Thessalonians chapter 3 and verses 6 through 10, it appears, based upon the direction of the text and the step of separation, withdraw yourselves, that these that were guilty simply weren't hearing the warning.
point_summary: The command to withdraw in 2 Thessalonians 3:6 represents a final disciplinary step taken only after repeated warnings had already been given and ignored by those walking disorderly.

point_summary: Paul's repeated request across multiple churches — that believers pray for the free course and glorification of the word — demonstrates this was a consistent apostolic petition, not an isolated appeal.

context: Paul says in 2 Thessalonians, it's very similar to what we read here in 2 Thessalonians 3. It's interesting, in 1 Thessalonians in chapter five, he simply says this, brethren, pray for us. context: finally, brethren, pray for us. What does he ask that they pray for? That the word of the Lord may have free course and be glorified, even as it is with you

context: Notice in 2 Thessalonians chapter number 3, 2 Thessalonians chapter number 3, and notice in verse number 14, this is very interesting because it's speaking about those that aren't obeying even the words of the epistle that is written to a local church. context: In 2 Thessalonians chapter number two, I mentioned already, verse number seven, where the Bible
point_summary: The mystery of iniquity already at work in Paul's day is cited as biblical grounding for the claim that the Antichrist's rise follows the rapture of the church.

context: let no man deceive you by any means, for that day shall not come, except there come a falling away first, and that man of sin be revealed, the son of perdition
point_summary: Paul's description of "that man of sin" and "son of perdition" in 2 Thessalonians 2:3 is identified as the Antichrist whose rise, following a falling away, is yet future and post-rapture.

context: who opposeth and exalteth himself above all that is called God, or that is worshiped, so that he as God sitteth in the temple of God, showing himself that he is God.

context: the Bible tells us that the charity of every one of you all toward each other aboundeth
point_summary: Paul's commendation of the Thessalonians—that the charity of every one of them toward each other abounded—is held up as the positive model of brotherly love against which the divided church at Corinth is contrasted.

context: as it was of the church in Thessalonica in 2 Thessalonians
point_summary: The church at Thessalonica is held up as a model where the charity of every one toward each other abounded, contrasting with Corinth's divisions and urging continual growth in love.

context: the charity of every one of you all toward each other aboundeth
point_summary: The phrase "the charity of every one of you all toward each other aboundeth" is held up as a model of love that transcends cliques and extends to every member of the congregation without exception.

context: we look here in 2 Thessalonians 2 and look at verse number 1
point_summary: Paul's authority as the human author of 2 Thessalonians is grounded in his total surrender to Christ, which the sermon presents as the foundation for understanding the epistle's message.

context: Notice what it says in verse number one of 2 Thessalonians chapter number two. The apostle Paul says this, now we beseech you brethren, by the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ

context: our gathering together unto him
point_summary: Paul's appeal in 2 Thessalonians 2:1 anchors the Thessalonians' instruction in two realities—the coming of Christ and the believers' gathering together unto Him—as the doctrinal foundation for what follows.

context: that you be not soon shaken in mind or be troubled
point_summary: Paul's corrective in verse 2 targets a specific deception circulating among the Thessalonians — false communications, including a forged letter claiming apostolic authority, asserting that the Day of Christ had already come.

context: Let no man deceive you by any means
point_summary: Before the Day of Christ arrives, Paul insists two events must precede it — a falling away and the revelation of the man of sin — correcting those who were falsely claiming that day had already come.

context: The Greek word that is used there is only used two times in all of the New Testament. And that often when you see something like that as I study and I prepare, as you see a particular word that is used just a one or two or three times, it's of interest. You want to find out where else that word might be used. And it was of great interest to me because here in 2 Thessalonians 2 and verse number one, the apostle Paul is writing to a local church and he's writing to a local church about a future event, our gathering
point_summary: The Greek word translated "gathering together" in 2 Thessalonians 2:1 appears only twice in the New Testament, linking this future eschatological event to its one other occurrence in Hebrews 10:25.

context: That's exactly the gathering that we're speaking about in 2 Thessalonians 2, in verse number one.
point_summary: The "gathering together unto him" in 2 Thessalonians 2:1 is identified with Christ's promised return to receive believers unto himself, as stated in John 14, distinguishing that future gathering from the present assembling of saints.

context: we go back to 2nd Thessalonians chapter number two In verse number one and the Bible says now we beseech you brethren notice by the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ
point_summary: The phrase "our gathering together unto Him" in 2 Thessalonians 2:1 frames the present corporate assembly of believers as preparation for the ultimate eschatological gathering at Christ's return.

context: by the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ notice this and by our gathering together unto him
point_summary: Paul's phrase "our gathering together unto him" in 2:1 is read as the definitive future event toward which every present assembly of believers is oriented and preparation.

context: I beseech you brethren by the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ and by our gathering together unto him
point_summary: Paul's phrase "our gathering together unto him" in 2 Thessalonians 2:1 anchors the Rapture's supreme significance in permanent union with Christ himself, not merely the event's mechanics.

context: Finally, brethren, pray for us, that the word of the Lord may have free course and be glorified, even as it is with you.
point_summary: Paul's specific prayer request in 2 Thessalonians 3:1 — that the word of the Lord have free course and be glorified — is illuminated by 1 Thessalonians 2:13, where the Thessalonians had already received that word as God's own.

context: In Second Thessalonians three, verse one, he talks about praying that the word of the Lord would have free course and be glorified even as it is with you.
point_summary: Paul's prayer request that the word of the Lord "have free course and be glorified" is grounded in the Thessalonians' own transformed lives as living proof that the gospel still works.

context: Verse two, and that we may be delivered from unreasonable and wicked men for all men have not faith.

context: We find that he says he's praying for deliverance from these unreasonable and wicked men for all men have not faith.
point_summary: Paul's request for prayer to be delivered from "unreasonable and wicked men" reflects his lived experience of fierce, persistent opposition to the gospel at every turn.

context: for the mystery of iniquity doth already work.
point_summary: The mystery of iniquity already at work in the world confirms that there is a real power of darkness from which believers must be delivered.

context: Notice in 2 Thessalonians chapter number two, we see this again.

context: as we come to the end of 2 Thessalonians 3, and we look at this portion of scripture, we find that Paul concludes this section of this book, he specifically addresses how to deal with those, according to verse number 14, who obey not our word by this epistle.

context: In verse number 14, the Bible says this, and if any man obey not our word by this epistle, note that man and have no company with him that he may be ashamed.
point_summary: Verse 14's command to "note" and withdraw company from the disobedient establishes the basis for church discipline, but the sermon frames this as a restorative measure, never an end in itself.

context: Verse 15, yet count him not as an enemy, but admonish him as a brother.
point_summary: Verse 15's contrast with verse 14 establishes that church discipline's ultimate aim is restoration, not excommunication, with the offender's response determining whether that restoration is achieved.

context: ...but ye brethren be not weary in well doing.
point_summary: Even as Paul addresses disorderly members, he charges the faithful not to grow weary in well-doing, calling the obedient to persevere rather than falter amid others' failures.

context: Remember back in verse number six, he said, now we command you, brethren, in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, that you withdraw yourselves from every brother that walketh disorderly, and not after the tradition which he received of us.
point_summary: Verse 6's command to "withdraw" from the disorderly brother establishes the biblical basis for the separation later reinforced in verses 14–15, showing the instruction is not extreme but apostolic.

context: when we look at the passage of Scripture in 2 Thessalonians 3 verses 14 and 15, we must understand this difference between restoration and excommunication.
point_summary: Church discipline in 2 Thessalonians 3:14—marking the disorderly and withdrawing fellowship—is purposefully restorative, not excommunicatory, reflecting God's own heart as a restorer of souls.

context: when we look at the passage of Scripture in 2 Thessalonians 3 verses 14 and 15, we must understand this difference between restoration and excommunication.
point_summary: Church discipline in 2 Thessalonians 3:14–15 is not excommunication but restoration, its purpose being shame that changes behavior and returns the wayward believer.

point_summary: Paul's expanded prayer request in 2 Thessalonians 3:1 adds specific content to the brief appeal of 1 Thessalonians 5:25, asking that the word of the Lord would have free course and be glorified.

context: pray that the word of the Lord would have free course
point_summary: Paul's request that the Thessalonians pray for the word's free course is grounded in their own experience of receiving that word not as human speech but as the very word of God that works effectually in believers.

context: here in 2 Thessalonians 3, we read in verse number one how that he made that request that the word of the Lord would be glorified and have free course, and be glorified even as is with you
point_summary: Paul's prayer request that the word of the Lord have free course and be glorified is paired with an appeal for deliverance from opposition, showing that gospel advance and spiritual warfare are inseparable realities.

context: but then in verse number two he says, and that we may be delivered from unreasonable and wicked men, for all men have not faith
point_summary: Paul's request for prayer against "unreasonable and wicked men" reflects his clear-eyed awareness that gospel ministry will face human opposition, making intercession a practical necessity for those doing the work.

context: But I want you to notice verse three of 2 Thessalonians chapter three. It says, but the Lord is faithful.
point_summary: God's faithfulness to establish and protect believers amid opposition is the bedrock assurance underlying Paul's call for prayer, not a substitute for it.

context: Notice here in 2 Thessalonians 3 and verse number 4, the Bible says, and we have confidence in the Lord touching you that ye both do and will do the things which we command you.
point_summary: Paul's confidence that the Thessalonians would follow through on his prayer request illustrates that knowing faithful intercessors are praying brings genuine encouragement to those doing gospel work.

context: 2 Thessalonians chapter 3, verses 6-18

context: 2 Thessalonians chapter number 3, beginning in verse number 6 and going through the end of the chapter

context: we're going to go over to 2 Thessalonians chapter 1
point_summary: The two letters to Thessalonica, written within months of each other, are placed side by side to show the congregation's measurable spiritual growth across that brief interval.

context: we are bound to thank God always for you, brethren, as it is me. Notice what he says, because that your faith, did you see what it says, groweth exceedingly
point_summary: Comparing 2 Thessalonians 1:3 with 1 Thessalonians 1:3 across a span of mere months, the Thessalonians' faith had not merely continued but was now growing exceedingly—demonstrating measurable spiritual growth.

context: so that we ourselves glory in you in the churches of God, notice for your patience and faith in all your persecutions and tribulations that ye endure.
point_summary: The Thessalonian church's patient endurance through persecutions and tribulations became a cause for Paul's boasting about them among other churches of God.

context: they dealt with the persecutions and the tribulations that are mentioned in 2 Thessalonians one and verse four
point_summary: Enduring persecution without quitting is cited as the mark of the Thessalonian church's faithfulness, with Paul's boasting about their patience and faith under tribulation serving as evidence they were a model church.

context: we mentioned that in 2 Thessalonians 1 and verse number 3, he talked about how your love or your charity of every one of you all toward each other aboundeth
point_summary: Paul's commendation that the Thessalonians' charity toward one another abounded is cited to show that even a model church is called to continual growth in love, never having fully arrived.

context: wherefore also we pray always for you
point_summary: Paul's consistent declaration "we pray always for you" to the Thessalonians is cited as one of several scriptural examples establishing his unceasing pattern of intercessory prayer for specific congregations.

reference_text: second Thessalonians chapter number three in verse number one
context: He says it again in second Thessalonians chapter number three in verse number one, he says, finally, brethren, pray for us.
point_summary: Paul's repeated appeal "pray for us" across his epistles is cited as a biblical pattern demonstrating that intercessory prayer was a mutual, reciprocal practice among believers, not a one-directional duty.

context: You look at 2 Thessalonians 2 and you see specifically that being dealt with
point_summary: False teaching about the day of the Lord—including a counterfeit letter attributed to Paul—had led some Thessalonian believers astray, requiring Paul's correction in 2 Thessalonians 2.

context: If you notice that in chapter one in verse number three, and what a blessed truth to see that there's a church, a body of believers, that their faith was growing.
point_summary: Commendation of the Thessalonian church's exceedingly growing faith serves as the positive backdrop against which the sermon's warning about false teaching and internal disorder is set.

context: for we hear, verse 11, that there are some which walk among you disorderly
point_summary: Idleness rooted in false eschatological teaching—the belief that the Day of the Lord had already come—produced the disorderly, busybody behavior Paul addresses in verse 11.

context: working not at all, but are busybodies
point_summary: Paul's command in verse 12 pairs the correction of idleness with a positive mandate: those who are disorderly must work quietly and provide their own sustenance, addressing both the behavior and its remedy.

context: for we hear that there are some which walk among you disorderly, working not at all, but are busybodies.
point_summary: Receiving Paul's rebuke about busybodies as a member of the Thessalonian church would have created a sharp, uncomfortable contrast with the earlier praise of their faith and love.

context: According to what we read in 2 Thessalonians chapter number 2 and 3, we begin to understand this

context: Now we beseech you, brethren, by the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ, and by
point_summary: Paul's warning in 2 Thessalonians 2 is introduced as the New Testament counterpart to Daniel 9:27, confirming that the man of sin will seat himself in a literal future temple at the tribulation's midpoint.

context: In chapter number 2 of the book of 2 Thessalonians
point_summary: Paul's description of the man of sin sitting in the temple of God is read as New Testament confirmation that a literal, rebuilt temple must exist during the tribulation's midpoint.

context: our gathering unto him, that you be not soon shaken in mind or troubled, neither by spirit nor by word nor by letter as from us, as that the day of Christ is at hand
point_summary: Paul's appeal to the Thessalonians concerning "the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ and our gathering unto him" is read as a direct parallel to Daniel's seventieth week, with the man of sin's self-enthronement in the temple marking the tribulation's midpoint.

context: Let no man deceive you by any means. For that day shall not come except there come a falling away first, and that man of sin be revealed, the son of perdition
point_summary: The Day of the Lord cannot come until the man of sin is first revealed — identified here as the one who seats himself in the temple of God at the tribulation's midpoint.

context: Who opposeth and exalteth himself above all that is called God, or that is worship, notice this, so that he as God sitteth in the temple of God, showing himself that he is God
point_summary: Paul's description of the man of sin enthroning himself in the temple of God is identified as the midpoint of the tribulation, when the covenant of Daniel 9:27 is broken.

context: wherefore also we pray always for you that our God would count you worthy of this calling and fulfill all the good pleasure of his goodness and the work of faith with power
point_summary: Paul's consistent intercession for the Thessalonians across two letters illustrates that persistent, ongoing prayer for others is a defining mark of apostolic ministry.

context: they'll be given that strong delusion that 2 Thessalonians talks about
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: For the mystery of iniquity doth already work. Only he who now letteth will let until he be taken out of the way.
point_summary: The Holy Spirit's present restraining of iniquity will be removed at the Rapture, after which the mystery of lawlessness will be unleashed during the Tribulation.

context: verse number 10, the Bible says, And with all deceivableness of unrighteousness in them that perish, notice, because they receive not the love of the truth, that they might be saved
point_summary: Those who reject the love of the truth during the tribulation will be given over to strong delusion by God himself, resulting in damnation — a consequence tied directly to the removal of the Holy Spirit's restraining work at the rapture.

context: if we believe what 2 Thessalonians says that I read just a moment ago, God's gonna send them strong delusion. They're gonna believe a lie.
point_summary: God's sending of strong delusion upon those who reject the gospel means that loved ones who have already heard and refused the message may have no further opportunity for salvation during the Tribulation, making present evangelism and intercession urgently necessary.

context: Look at 2 Thessalonians just quickly, and then we'll look at a couple of verses there in the book of 1 John. In 2 Thessalonians chapter two.
point_summary: Paul's letter to the Thessalonians is presented as a corrective to false teaching—including forged letters bearing Paul's name—that had shaken the church's understanding of end-time events, including the Antichrist's revelation.

context: chapter three and verse 17
point_summary: Paul's practice of personally signing his letters (3:17) is cited as the authenticating mark that exposed forged correspondence falsely attributed to him, which had shaken the Thessalonian church.

context: verse number two and three from chapter number two
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: now we beseech you brethren, by the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ and by our gathering together unto him, that ye be not soon shaken in mind or be troubled, neither by spirit nor by word, notice this, nor by letter as from us, as that the day of Christ is at hand
point_summary: Paul's appeal in 2 Thessalonians 2:1 addresses a congregation shaken by forged letters claiming his authority, urging them not to be troubled concerning the coming of Christ and the gathering together unto Him.

context: let no man deceive you by any means
point_summary: The Day of Christ cannot arrive until the Antichrist — the man of sin — is first revealed, an event the sermon ties to his covenant-signing in Daniel 9:27.

context: that the day of Christ is at hand
point_summary: False teaching is not new, but Paul's warning in this verse against being shaken or troubled by spurious letters grounds the sermon's point that deception precedes the Day of Christ and the Antichrist's revelation.

context: the man of sin be revealed the son of perdition
point_summary: The Day of Christ cannot arrive until the Antichrist—the man of sin—is first revealed, an event identified here with his signing of the covenant described in Daniel 9:27.

context: in 2 Thessalonians chapter number three, excuse me, chapter two, it says this in verse number six, and now you know what withholdeth that he might be revealed in his time, for the mystery of iniquity doth already work
point_summary: The restraining presence described in 2 Thessalonians 2:6 is identified as the Holy Spirit, whose removal allows the Antichrist to be revealed in his time.

context: if you were to look at 2nd Thessalonians 2 and verse number one where Paul says, now we beseech you brethren by the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ and by our gathering together unto him
point_summary: Paul's phrase "our gathering together unto him" in 2 Thessalonians 2:1 is cited as a direct reference to Christ coming *for* his saints, distinguished from his later coming *with* his saints.

context: 2nd Thessalonians chapter number two, I read verse number one
point_summary: Paul's phrase "our gathering together unto him" in 2 Thessalonians 2:1 is used to distinguish the rapture—Christ coming *for* his saints—from his later return *with* his saints at Armageddon.

context: now we beseech you brethren by the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ and by our gathering together unto him that you be not soon shaken in mind or be troubled
point_summary: Paul's appeal to "our gathering together unto him" in 2 Thessalonians 2:1 is used to establish a distinction between Christ coming *for* His saints and coming *with* His saints at a later point.

context: Let no man deceive you by any means for that day shall not come except there come a falling away first and that man of sin be revealed the son of perdition
point_summary: Paul's identification of "the falling away" and the revelation of "the man of sin" in 2 Thessalonians 2:3 is connected directly to the midpoint of Daniel's 70th week, when the Antichrist exalts himself in the temple.

context: who opposeth and exalteth himself above all that is called God that is worshiped so that he as God sitteth in the temple of God showing himself that he is God
point_summary: Paul's identification of the man of sin enthroning himself in the temple and claiming to be God directly corresponds to the abomination described in Daniel 9:27, confirming the midpoint desecration of the tribulation period.

context: remember ye not that when I was yet with you, I told you these things
point_summary: Paul's reminder in 2 Thessalonians 2:5 — "I told you these things" — establishes that his teaching on the man of sin and the tribulation was not new but previously delivered doctrine the Thessalonians had forgotten.

context: ye know what withholdeth that he might be revealed in his time
point_summary: Paul's statement that "ye know what withholdeth" presupposes prior teaching, connecting the restraining of the man of sin's revelation to the timing of Daniel's 70th week.

context: for the mystery of iniquity doth already work, only he who now letteth will let until he be taken out of the way
point_summary: The restraining work of the Holy Spirit—"he who now letteth"—is identified as the divine force currently holding back the full revelation of the man of sin until God's appointed time.

context: only he who now letteth will let until he be taken out of the way
point_summary: The Holy Spirit, identified as the restrainer of 2 Thessalonians 2:7, indwells believers today, and His removal at the Rapture is what permits the Antichrist to operate freely during Daniel's 70th week.

context: throughout the book of 1 and 2 Thessalonians, are being commended for their faithfulness and their obedience to the truth of God's word
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: in the beginning of 2 Thessalonians, it's really interesting to see how they're commended
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: according to 2 Thessalonians chapter number three, are gonna be deceived, they're gonna believe a lie
point_summary: Those who heard the gospel before the Rapture but rejected it will, according to 2 Thessalonians 3, be given over to deception and believe a lie during the Tribulation.
